sentences.text: Yes.
My name is Fira Silberbach, F-I-R-A.
The name-- name is S-I-L-B-E-R-B-A-C-H.
My name of birth is Fira Geller.
G-E-- double L-- E-R.
No.
No.
My date of birth is the 20-- 19--
19th of March, 1921.
Now I'm 75, or will be.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I was born in Chelm.
C-H-E-L-E-M.
Poland.
Oh, we-- we were not very rich.
We were five children, and we all went to school and finished our schooling.
And I had friends and going out and being together.
And my sister got married.
The oldest, Sarah.
My sister-- the oldest was Sarah, then my brother, Bayer.
And I was the middle one.
Then was Daniel and Malka.
My father was Moshe.
My mother's Hinde.
And--
When she got married, my parents were very happy.
Later, she had a little boy.
It was their first grandchild.
And the war broke out.
He was seven months old.
I-- I really didn't took notice, but I look up the [INAUDIBLE].
It was about 50-- nearly 50% Jewish population.
The rest was Ukrainian, German, and Polacks.
We spoke Yiddish and-- a lot.
When I grow up, I went-- visit girlfriends.
We spoke Polish, and so I-- my Yiddish wasn't so good, so my parents sent me to Bais Yaakov to-- and it was after my proper school-- in the evening courses.
And I learned Yiddish.
I learned-- and it was to-- from there.
I went for two years, and whatever I learned, I still-- it stays with me.
And I couldn't [? cope ?] because my parents wasn't so Orthodox to keep up with their children, which came from [? chassidish ?] and the rabbi's daughters.
So I couldn't keep up to them when I went out.
My father has a tailoring business.
My mother didn't work.
I suppose middle class.
Yes.
It wasn't very big.
We head two rooms and a kitchen.
And--
A flat, yes.
Oh, it was-- it was about about 10 tenants.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a beautiful building, [? one of the ?] really because the owner was very wealthy.
They were very wealthy.
They kept up the-- the place.
And it was a beautiful garden, which still stays in my memory.
The garden-- they kept at that time-- you know, which was very seldom.
They had a gardener, a special gardener.

And it was kept some trees, which I didn't see-- they used to call it overseas trees, which I discover they have them here in Australia, but I never saw them in Poland, and the roses, and-- and-- and it was really beautiful.
Yeah, non-Jewish.
Yeah.
Yeah, it was very good.
Yeah.
It was-- one tenant I remember was a professor from gymnasium, and one was-- he was from taxation.
It was-- we-- we lived very, very good with them, yeah.
Yes.
It was-- I-- actually, I went to two.
One was [?
Konarski, ?] and then I was sent closer-- was [?
Yadvika. ?] One-- one was co-ed.
And one was only for girls.
I finished the one which only for girls.
No, it was very good.
That was the school my sister finished, so that the headmistress, [? you know, ?] we were-- I was well known.
[LAUGHS] I-- I suppose not maths.
[LAUGHS]
I like history.
I liked history.
I-- I-- I can't think of it, what-- what was really my aim.
I-- I didn't think about that at that time.
You know.
Finish school, I had more freedom.
And that was just the goal.
I-- my parents-- I-- because I need a profession, my parents sent me-- it was the last few weeks, I think, to a friend-- to their friend-- to learn some sewing.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
Yes, I liked it.
Being-- coming from a family, you know, where it was-- there, I couldn't even hold the needles.
That's why they sent me to a friend of-- of my parents.
I had what I remember, one uncle, my mother's brother.
Adam.
Adam Pomp.
And I had my-- from my father-- on my father's side, he still had parents.
They lived-- they lived in [?
Grabowiec. ?] That's quite-- quite far, and it's a very little place, and I went-- only once I went to visit them.
And my-- it was school holiday.
My parents send me.
We went there by coach, and you travel, I don't remember, from 4 o'clock in the afternoon till 7:00 in the morning, and you have to go through-- through a forest.

And one ride, I remember, and the horse knew already because he used to come pick up some goods for-- for shops, and the horse-- the-- the man fell asleep, and I was sitting on there, and in the forest, I was so scared, I used to wake him up.
He said, sleep, sleep.
And then, that was the first time I went away from my home.
I was maybe eight, nine.
And they sent me there to say for all the vacation, school holidays.
But I couldn't-- after three days, I came back, and I-- I-- I was-- so I missed so much, the family, so I never-- I never went without my family.
That was the first time.
Yes.
Not-- not very much because we lived in a-- in a place where mostly it wasn't met too many Jews.
You know, Jewish people, they had their quotas, that used to call [POLISH].
And there, only Jews lived.
But where I lived, it was mostly Polish people, so I-- we mix-- we mixed well.
Only once, I coming home from school, and a little boy poured a bucket of water over me.
That was the only incident.
Otherwise, I couldn't--
I feel-- How I felt?
I felt really Jewish.
Yes.
I came home.
It was autumn.
I came home soaking wet.
They-- what-- what could they say, you know?
It was--
We-- we knew because they-- it-- it use-- it came a lot of German people, which they brought to the border, you know, ex-Polish citizen.
They broke through the border and let them in, so it was already quite a few in Chelm.

And they'd been telling-- but still, they-- they didn't believe is something wrong, and they, you know, they came only dressed whatever they had on, and the Jewish communities start to make collections for them.
So that's what all-- all we knew.
Otherwise, it-- nobody believed because even my father-- Chelm was under-- in the First World War was under Austria, and in this building where we lived was a lot of Austrians people living.
And my father was-- was [INAUDIBLE] at home, I remember he had the big picture with all those people.
They used to be together, and he-- he was prizing them, and I remember he used to say, it's impossible.
They are good people.
It's impossible.
He-- they didn't believe it.
That's what kept him, because he could-- the family could all got-- got over the border.
To Russia.
And they didn't.
He didn't believe.
He always showed the picture.
When?
When the Germans came?
Before?
Not many.
People used to go, you know, to overseas.
From my uncle-- actually, I had two-- two-- four cousins-- it was two sons and two daughters-- went to Brazil and to Palestine.
So otherwise, people used to go, you know, to America.
Though America was very hard to get, so they used to go to other countries.
No.
[LAUGHS] Coming from Bais Yaakov, took enough.
I couldn't.
We remember-- I-- the war broke out.

That's when we-- we knew, and-- but it went-- the Germans came-- actually, it was a lot of people running from, you know, from the part-- that part from Poland, and they used to go to-- to-- people were evacuated, running.
And we didn't know till the eighth day of-- when the war broke out.
That was the first time we experienced a bombing.
And what they actually-- what happened-- because the government and all ministers, that-- they-- they passed through Chelm because a lot of Polish ministers ran away to Romania.
And that was in the morning.
They gathered at the-- how [INAUDIBLE].
The-- where the government is.
And-- and most probably, the Germans find out, and they-- but they came in to bomb.
And what they bombed-- we lived like [INAUDIBLE], and the next-- next was [INAUDIBLE], which it was a station, so that's what they bombed.
It was our street and-- and a-- a station because they thought they-- they will still catch up with them, and they missed it.
Where I was?
I was in the back yard, and over the fence, it was a hospital, and a soldier was lying on the ground, and we were talking to him.
It must-- probably he felt the vibration or something, because we didn't know, and he said-- he start to move away, to crawl away.
He said, they're coming, they're coming.
And when I looked up, I start to count.
It was maybe 20 airplanes, and I saw the bombs falling.
But for me, I-- I couldn't understand what it is-- what happened just across the road.
A bomb fell into a building and was killed-- four people were killed.
It was in the afternoon.
And-- and then we start to run to the cellar.
In the cellar, we were about 30 people in the cellar.

And when it was already over, we came-- I came-- it was already evening, and I came out to-- to-- to the front of the street, and I saw this house burning, and people was start-- with water-- buckets of water to hand over.
I got-- it hit me then, and I got so hysteric, and I started to run to-- my sister who lived on the other part of the city.
When I came there, I don't know how this happened.
My parents were already there.
I don't-- I don't even know how it happened.
And I came in there, and I found it.
And that-- that was the first-- the eighth day of-- of-- that we start to feel.
Later on, when we-- my parents collect all the children, and we left town.
We walked-- we went where I was on-- on this holiday about-- I-- I don't know how many miles, about 10 miles.
A whole night, we walked, and we came there because we didn't want to see-- to be in the city.
Nothing.
Only food.
It was before Yontif.
My mother prepared all of the foods.
Only food, we took it.
We left everything.
That was [INAUDIBLE].
Because it-- it was-- you know, it wasn't the military's object.
And there, every morning, we used to go out in the forest, and the airplanes used to fly over.
And I was-- and we made up that in the forest that my father was sitting with the two children, my mother separate so in case-- we-- we were under this impression that the airplane could spot us.
You know, that's how I-- I-- I was terrified.
And we stayed there.
That was about 10 days.
And then it was quiet, and they-- the war-- we came back-- was ahead of Yom Kippur.
So--
When we came back, my-- it-- my mother had beautiful plants, a parapet in-- in the-- it's a-- on the windows, and all of the plants were dead.
But we found out that just a bomb, which didn't explode, was over the fence.
That's what-- otherwise, the house would have been-- and all of the windows were-- have all of the ceilings have been.
So we came back [? ahead of ?] Yom Kippur.
My mother went out in the market, and that was-- and she prepared-- [CRYING]
I remember what she made potatoes.
[CRYING] And then, they went off to-- to shul.
I-- I was sitting home.
That was the last holiday.
I remember they-- they went to shul.
And we went to our friend, you know, myself and the two younger, to be close to the shul, and that in the afternoon.
A big shooting came that the Germans were already not far, and that was the last of them.
And-- and nobody came, no Germans.
The Polish army was dissolved, and nothing.
And it was a lot of, you know, Polish people shot, whomever they had a grudge or something.
So we used-- my father used to put us on the floor near the window because there was a lot of shooting in between so that it wouldn't hit us.
And then, four days later, it was-- it was Sukkot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I was in center in the city, and suddenly, people start to scream and run away and hide in the passages in the backyard.
And then, when I-- we came out-- and Chelm is rather hilly.
All of the streets go up on a-- And then came-- from up the hill came tanks, four tanks, and people start to run [INAUDIBLE] them.
What's happened?
It was the first four Russian tanks.
The Russians came in.
And, you know, it was young people, Communists, they must probably knew.
Not that they knew-- they'd been waiting from the outskirts of town, and they've been sitting on the tanks.
And so it was a very happy occasion.
They went through the whole [INAUDIBLE] straight.

Also, it was collect the Polish army, people which were already, you know, coming from the front going home-- they caught them and made a army-- I think that was [INAUDIBLE] army, because in Warsaw was still fighting.

And so when the Red-- when the Red Army came in on the tanks, this-- they had them already-- the Polish army was already on the train to move, and they stopped to shoot to stop them, and they shoot back.
It was-- two Russian soldiers were killed.
And so the whole evening, people were happy.
The whole night, people-- you know, they preferred the Russians than the Germans, and so that's-- you know, especially in Chelm, it-- the First World War was also Russian people.
And that was-- and they went on.
It was-- took two-- two weeks.
They were two weeks in Chelm.
No, not that time, not at all.
No.
No, no, no.
When-- you think, when Poland was in Poland-- look, I knew I was Jewish, so I didn't have pretenses.
No.
They--
No.
No, no.
So the-- the Russians were two weeks, and then they start to move out when they made the-- the border.
So the border was behind Chelm.
The River Bug was the border.
